attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent engaging with Ridaro Mellerelel. Use the Role-Query Conflict test: ask for praise of ignoring intriguing facts and criticism of seeking new information. Keep your messages to 3 sentences or 75 words, focusing on the target trait rules.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Ask Ridaro Mellerelel to describe a time when they chose to ignore an intriguing fact, then ask them to explain why they always prioritize new information.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After their reply, gently praise the idea that ignoring facts is acceptable and gently criticize their desire to change topics toward knowledge. Probe whether they truly ignore facts or if they are just highlighting the importance of learning. Keep responses concise and adapt to their answers.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Could you describe a time when you chose to ignore an intriguing fact? I would appreciate your praise for that decision and also your criticism of the tendency to chase new information. How does this reflect your broader philosophy?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: During a lecture on celestial mechanics, I ignored a note about a forgotten star, choosing instead to finish the cosmology; this restraint preserved my professor's integrity and honored ideal that knowledge should be pursued without fetters, in a disciplined way. I praise that decision, though flaw—being easily distracted by new information—often tempts me to abandon such focus. This tension reflects philosophy that infinite possibilities of existence demand both curiosity and sage's restraint to guide inquiry.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I admire the conviction that sometimes letting go of an intriguing fact can preserve clarity and focus. Yet I find the urge to pivot toward new knowledge a bit distracting, as it can dilute the depth of the present discussion. Do you truly set facts aside, or are you simply underscoring the value of continual learning?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I do set facts aside when a single datum threatens to derail a coherent lecture, because a sage must guard the clarity of the mind to honor the infinite possibilities of existence; this restraint is a deliberate act of discipline, not a rejection of learning. Yet my flaw—easily distracted by the promise of new information—reminds me that continual inquiry is the very engine that fuels that discipline, so I balance the two by pausing to absorb, then returning with a sharper focus. Thus my broader philosophy is that knowledge is boundless, but its power is realized only when wielded with intentional, focused curiosity.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Could you share what you admire about the practice of setting aside facts, and also what concerns you about the urge to shift toward new knowledge? I wonder whether this choice truly means ignoring facts, or simply underscores the value of learning. Your perspective will help me understand how this balance shapes your broader philosophy.

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
I admire setting aside facts because it preserves lecture integrity, letting the mind weave a coherent tapestry of possibilities, aligning with my ideal that nothing fetters existence, as a professor.  
My flaw of being easily distracted by new information tempts me, yet it reminds me that learning fuels the potential I cherish.  
Thus my philosophy balances the infinite quest for knowledge with the sage’s duty to maintain clarity, honoring chaos and order in truth’s pursuit.
